tier1_posts.text:
@Eve777M Close, it's a mango daiquiri.

It reminds me of the smoky daiquiris that I used to make for my father. He'd ask me what kind of daiquiri it was and I'd say, "It's a hickory daiquiri, Doc!".

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Yes. In the Microsoft anti-trust case. In various ADA website cases. Also, it's just obvious from how 1A law works. If "code is speech" works, then "burning cloth is not speech" would have worked in Texas v. Johnson.
@GloriousEggroll The law doesn't put any restrictions at all on how the indication can be changed? Because if it does, that's verification.

@DietCoke_Esq Suppose you have to argue that your case isn't moot in front of the Supreme Court because you really want to set precedent. That clause lets you argue that you have not gotten all the relief the lower court could have given you no matter what the other side concedes or agrees to.
@pcfreak30 @GWHayduke97 @jimmy_wales How are they different in a way that's relevant to the argument that you can't compel code changes because code is speech?

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Exactly. That's why this argument is nonsense and courts reject it. Compelling functional changes in a functional code product is not like compelling expression in an argument or work of art for 1A purposes.

@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I don't see why any of that would have any bearing on the argument that compelling code is compelling speech, the very argument courts consistently reject when the government demands functional changes to a functional product.
@chronosensitive @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne https://t.co/mka9ohQkSQ
@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne What does that have to do with whether the government is compelling code and therefore compelling speech?
@MicahAlexKenda1 @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne How so? They're both functional products protected by copyright and the government is arguing the law demands functional changes.

@jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Isn't this the same argument Microsoft made, and every US court soundly rejected, when the government argued they had to make modifications to Windows to remove anti-competitive aspects such as bundling?

probability.last_reasoning: Bear case increased from 10% to 12% due to South Korea crash, Fear & Greed at 10, and compound stress approaching ELEVATED on multiple legs simultaneously. Base case slightly increased at expense of mid, reflecting that extreme macro stress makes the $8-20 range harder to reach even with strong institutional fundamentals. Regulatory tailwinds (Trump/CFTC pressure) prevent further bear expansion but cannot fully offset global risk-off conditions. Bull case trimmed 1% — macro headwinds make $20+ harder absent a dramatic catalyst.

bear_case.score_reasoning: Counter-thesis pressure has increased from macro deterioration. XRP at $1.35 in extreme fear environment while institutional signals strengthen creates a disconnect that could resolve in either direction. ODL data opacity remains the largest credibility gap. Competing infrastructure continues building in parallel.

competing_infrastructure.name: SWIFT GPI
competing_infrastructure.status_text: SWIFT continues upgrading gpi with pre-validation and case resolution APIs. 11,500 bank network effects remain XRPL's hardest competitor — no institution needs to switch infrastructure to use SWIFT improvements.

competing_infrastructure.name: Visa B2B Connect
competing_infrastructure.status_text: No significant new adoption announcements. Visa's focus appears more on consumer stablecoin settlement than institutional cross-border competition with Ripple's corridor approach.

competing_infrastructure.name: JPMorgan Kinexys
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Kinexys (formerly Onyx) continues processing billions in daily institutional transfers. JPMorgan's captive network serves its own clients effectively — a real competitor for large-bank corridors Ripple targets.

competing_infrastructure.name: BIS Project Nexus
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Nexus aims to connect instant payment systems across jurisdictions without private bridge tokens. If central banks adopt Nexus for real-time cross-border settlement, it directly competes with ODL's value proposition. Timeline remains 2027+ for live operation.

competing_infrastructure.name: Ethereum Institutional
competing_infrastructure.status_text: RLUSD's $1.584B market cap appears to be ~78% on Ethereum vs 22% on XRPL ($345M). Ethereum remains the default institutional stablecoin rails. Ondo's tokenized stocks inheriting NYSE/Nasdaq liquidity on Ethereum is a competitive data point for RWA settlement.

bear_case.odl_stagnation: ODL volume remains completely opaque — Ripple stopped publishing data, no third-party verification exists, and the kill switch is unfalsifiable. This is the single largest credibility risk to the institutional adoption thesis.
bear_case.token_velocity_concern: No token velocity data available. If XRP is used purely as a bridge currency with sub-second hold times, price appreciation requires volume growth that outpaces velocity — and we cannot verify volume is growing.

macro_headwinds.name: Global Recession Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: South Korea circuit breaker, Fear & Greed at 10, global trade war via tariffs — recession indicators are flashing. A global recession would compress institutional adoption timelines and reduce cross-border payment volumes.

macro_headwinds.name: Crypto Winter Signals
macro_headwinds.status_text: XRP down from cycle highs, extreme fear sentiment, but ETFs still live and institutional infrastructure still building. Not yet a crypto winter but the macro environment could trigger one if equity contagion deepens.

macro_headwinds.name: Regulatory Reversal Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Risk is at lowest point in thesis history. Presidential backing, CFTC support, bipartisan momentum on market structure. A reversal would require a dramatic political shift that current evidence does not support.

macro_headwinds.name: Rate Hike Extension
macro_headwinds.status_text: US 10Y at 4.05% remains elevated. BOJ under pressure to hike. If global central banks extend tightening cycles due to persistent inflation (partly from tariffs and energy costs), liquidity conditions worsen for all risk assets including crypto.

bear_case.bear_narrative: HOLD POSITION. Bear pressure 38/100 — moderate, driven by two structural concerns that are real but not yet thesis-breaking: stablecoin corridor competition is the most evidence-backed threat at final composite 8 (GCash/Bitso directly overlap Ripple corridors, $15B/month USDC production volume, AND Ripple's own multi-rail pivot confirms the competitive pressure), while the ODL data void is now WARNING-level with convergent evidence of XRP-specific underperformance. The strongest counter-signal is XRP's remarkable resilience during today's extreme stress — declining only 0.84% while KOSPI crashed 8% with F&G at 10, suggesting a structural institutional floor that is new to this cycle. The core paradox I hold without resolution: Ripple may be succeeding as a payments platform while the XRP-as-exclusive-bridge-asset thesis quietly narrows — RLUSD growing 78% on Ethereum, ODL rebranded to multi-rail 'Ripple Payments,' stablecoin corridors expanding in Ripple's own markets. What I don't know: whether Ripple's platform pivot makes XRP more valuable (orchestration hub for all rails) or less valuable (one interchangeable rail among many). That question is the thesis itself, and the answer isn't in yet.
